summary:
The Siege of Cawnpore (now Kanpur) was one of the most traumatic episodes of the [[event:2562|Indian Rebellion of 1857]] for the British community in India. A British garrison of about 1,000 soldiers, civilians, and their families endured a three-week siege by rebel forces under Nana Sahib before negotiating a safe passage by river. The subsequent killing of the British survivors — a massacre whose precise circumstances remain disputed by historians — became a defining atrocity in British memory of the rebellion.

The British garrison at Cawnpore surrendered on 27 June 1857 under a guarantee of safe conduct to Allahabad by boat. As they were boarding the boats at the Sati Chura Ghat, violence broke out — whether by accident, treachery, or misunderstanding is debated — and most of the male soldiers were killed. The surviving women and children, approximately 200 people, were held prisoner. When a British relief force approached in mid-July, the prisoners were massacred and their bodies thrown into a well.

The Bibighar massacre, as it became known, had an enormous psychological impact on British India. The image of British women and children killed by 'native' rebels was used to justify ferocious retribution in the reconquest of northern India. British forces executed captured rebels by tying them over cannon — a deliberate ritual humiliation for Hindus and Muslims alike. The events at Cawnpore became a central narrative in Victorian Britain's understanding of the rebellion and of India more broadly, feeding racist attitudes and hardening the cultural distance between rulers and ruled that characterized the [[event:2563|British Raj]] for the remainder of its existence.
